exemplar.title:
To what extent the awareness of female and male students of Kolej MARA Banting
towards consumption of plant-based milk having less impact on soil erosion compared to animal-based milk?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent the awareness of female and male students of Kolej MARA Banting
towards consumption of plant-based milk having less impact on soil erosion compared to animal-based milk?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Clear section headings and consistent use of variable tables and figure labels
Appropriate environmental and statistical terminology is used correctly
weaknesses: Inconsistent heading levels, caption styles, and occasional typos disrupt the flow
Verbose paragraphs and repeated wording reduce concision
Logical gaps between some sections hinder overall coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure and terminology are present but not sustained or concise, and argumentation is mainly logical with occasional gaps. This aligns with the descriptors for a band-2 rating in communication.

strengths: Clear research question with evident variables and scope
Provides contextual framing of soil erosion linked to dairy vs plant-based milk
Cites relevant sources to support the environmental context
weaknesses: Cumbersome phrasing and minor ambiguity in the research question
Lacks localized or Malaysian-specific data to deepen context
Connections between the environmental issue and question are described but not fully systematic
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands consistently sit in the mid-band (3â4): the question is relevant but phrased awkwardly, context is outlined but not in depth, and connections are described with some omissions. The work meets the descriptors for band 3 without demonstrating the comprehensive detail required for band 5â6.

strengths: Well-designed, repeatable survey method with pilot testing incorporated
Variables are clearly identified and a Likert coding system is established
Sampling strategy is justified using the Central Limit Theorem
weaknesses: Insufficient detail on survey distribution channels, follow-up reminders, and data cleaning
Randomization procedure and handling of non-response bias are not explained
Risk assessment omits data security, backup procedures, and deeper ethical safeguards
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design is appropriate and repeatable, sampling choice is justified, and ethical considerations are outlinedâdemonstrating substantial planning quality characteristic of the upper mid-band. Although some procedural details are missing, the core design secures a band-5 rating.

strengths: Thoughtful evaluation of methodological strengths (standardized questions, pilot testing) and weaknesses
Reasoned discussion of sample size limitations and their impact on generalizability
Links findings back to literature when evaluating conclusions
weaknesses: Evaluation of the conclusion in its broader environmental context lacks quantitative depth
Improvement suggestions are meaningful but lack methodological specificity (e.g., power analysis)
Omission of discussion around Likert-scale biases (central tendency, acquiescence)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student evaluates the method thoroughly and discusses limitations with insight, while conclusion evaluation and improvement proposals remain at a descriptive rather than exhaustive level. This performance corresponds to the 3â4 band.

strengths: Correct application of descriptive statistics and an independent-samples t-test
Data are presented with pie/bar charts and error bars for key comparisons
Conclusion directly references statistical outcomes and accepts the null hypothesis based on data
weaknesses: Charts lack full axis labels, legends, and complete raw data presentation
Statistical assumptions (normality, homogeneity of variance) are not tested or reported
Inconsistencies in sample size reporting and percentage denominators create confusion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student constructs appropriate diagrams with some omissions and conducts a correct but incomplete data analysis. Conclusions are supported by the t-test, placing the work squarely in the 3â4 band without fully meeting the requirement for comprehensive pattern analysis and assumption testing needed for band 5â6.

strengths: Application proposal is linked to survey findings (e.g., 87% support)
Identifies pertinent strengths and weaknesses of the proposed awareness campaign
weaknesses: Evaluation omits cultural acceptability, nutritional trade-offs, and cost analysis
Justification remains largely qualitative without quantitative grounding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student justifies one potential solution based on findings and evaluates some strengths and weaknesses, meeting the descriptors for a band-3 performance but falling short of a fully comprehensive evaluation required for the top of the scale.

strandSelections.reasoning: Environmental and statistical terminology (e.g., âstandard deviationâ, ât-testâ) is used correctly, yet the report is verbose with repeated wording and informal phrasing. This meets either concision or terminology consistently, but not both.

The student comments on how limited sample size affects generalisability and relates findings to literature. This is a reasoned evaluation but is not exhaustive; environmental implications are briefly noted without quantitative depth.

The conclusion explicitly refers to the t-test result and states acceptance of the null hypothesis. It is logically supported by the data presented, but wider uncertainties (sampling error, questionnaire reliability) are not incorporated, so the strand fits the 3-4 level.

Descriptive statistics (means, SDs) and an independent-samples t-test are correctly calculated; interpretation is mostly sound. Yet, assumptions (normality, equal variance) are not checked and analysis is confined to one test. Hence the analysis is correct but incomplete.

Proposed improvements include expanding demographic coverage and adding other colleges; these address major weaknesses but extensions remain general and lack methodological specifics such as revised sampling frames or alternative instruments.

The survey method is laid out with variables, full question list and Likert coding, enabling repetition. Nevertheless, procedures for distribution, data cleaning and statistical testing lack step-by-step detail, and raw data for only part of the sample are shown. Thus the method is repeatable and broadly appropriate but not developed enough for the top band.

Connections between the environmental issue and the question are described â e.g., dairy grazing â soil erosion â student awareness. Some causal pathways are mentioned (overgrazing, land use) but the explanation is not fully systematic and contains omissions (no quantitative erosion data, no clear rationale for studying this specific college). Hence the strand merits a descriptive (not fully comprehensive) level.

The student formulates a clear comparative question (gender-based awareness of plant- vs animal-milk impacts on soil erosion). Although the wording is cumbersome and contains grammatical flaws, the intent, variables and scope are evident, giving a focused direction for the study. Minor ambiguity in phrasing (âto what extent the awareness â¦ towards consumptionâ) prevents it from being fully coherent at the top band.

Strengths (reduced land pressure) and weaknesses (funding challenges in a developing country) of the solution are discussed, but several relevant dimensions (cultural acceptability, nutritional aspects) are omitted, indicating partial rather than full evaluation.

Clear section headings, variable tables and figure labels give the report a recognisable structure, but long unbroken paragraphs and some duplicated numbering disrupt consistency. Organisation is present though not sustained flawlessly.

Strengths (standardised questions, pilot test) and weaknesses (demographic variables, bias) are described with explanation of their impact on reliability. Most limitations are covered, though not every source of error (e.g., Likert-scale interpretation) is addressed.

The introduction outlines soil erosion, links it to grazing for dairy and to lower land demand for plant-based milk, and cites several sources. While brief, this provides sufficient contextual framing for the research question. The discussion remains descriptive and does not examine local-scale data in depth, so it sits comfortably in the mid-band rather than the most detailed level.

The student states that random sampling via Google Forms was used, sets a minimum of 30 per gender and justifies this with the Central Limit Theorem. However, there is no evidence of how the randomisation was operationalised or how non-response bias was handled. The choice is described but not fully justified, matching the 3-4 band.

One technical risk and two ethical considerations (informed consent, anonymity) are outlined. Coverage is basic and lacks depth on data security or psychological harm, so it is an outline rather than a comprehensive description.

The IA includes pie/bar charts for each section and means with error bars. Graphs are appropriate but some axes are unlabelled, titles are vague and only 20 of 61 raw responses are displayed. These omissions place the work in the âappropriate with some omissionsâ band.

The student proposes a government-led awareness campaign modelled on Denmarkâs action plan and links it to survey data (87 % support). Justification is present but largely qualitative and not deeply rooted in the studyâs findings, fitting the mid-band.

Arguments generally follow a logical flow from data â analysis â conclusion. Nevertheless, occasional logical gaps (e.g., switching from mean scores to campaign proposals without clear linkage) make some sections hard to follow, preventing a fully coherent rating.

annotations.comment: The research question expresses clear variables but the phrasing is cumbersome. Reword for clarityâe.g., âTo what extent do male and female students at Kolej MARA Banting differ in their awareness that plant-based milk has less impact on soil erosion than animal-based milk?â
While the question is focused, specifying âawarenessâ of what exactly (knowledge, attitudes, behavior) would tighten the scope. Clarify the dimension of awareness being investigated.
The introduction outlines soil erosion conceptually but lacks local or Malaysian-specific data. Incorporate regional erosion statistics or case studies to deepen contextual framing.
Bar Chart 1 communicates land use but axes lack labels and units. Add clear axis titles and units (e.g., million ha) for precise interpretation.
The literature cited is descriptive but relies on broad global studies. Strengthen context with more recent local publications or Malaysian government reports.
Typographical error in the answer key: âHugh awarenessâ should be âHigh awareness.â Such errors can confuse respondents and affect data quality.
The comprehensive list of 21 survey questions is thorough, but presenting them in one table is dense. Consider summarizing sections by theme to improve readability.
Random sampling via Google Forms is noted, but the procedure for randomization and handling non-response bias is not described. Add details on how equal chance was operationalized.
Pilot testing is a strength, showing proactive error checking. Note, however, how feedback informed specific question revisions to demonstrate its impact.
Risk section lists only platform glitches. Incorporate data security and backup procedures to address technical risks comprehensively.
Ethical considerations recognise informed consent and privacy, but data storage and anonymization protocols are missing. Specify how data will be securely stored and accessed.
Presenting 20 of 61 raw responses avoids length but omits summary of full data. Use descriptive summaries (means, medians) to present all respondentsâ patterns.
The variables table identifies independent, dependent, and constants, but the handling of constants (e.g., education level) needs clearer justification. Explain why education was held constant.
Hypotheses are stated, but the null and alternative arenât distinguished explicitly at this stage. Label Hâ and Hâ clearly and state them in conventional form.
The survey method section outlines question structure, but lacks details on distribution channels, follow-up reminders, and data cleaning procedures. Include these steps for full repeatability.
Chart captions are inconsistent (e.g., Pie Chart 1 vs Pie Chart 2 headings). Standardize caption style to maintain coherence throughout presentation.
Solution evaluation notes funding challenges but omits discussion of cultural or nutritional barriers to plant-based adoption in Malaysia. Address these for completeness.
Repeated long paragraphs and occasional typos (e.g., missing hyphens) disrupt concision. Break into shorter paragraphs and proofread for clarity.
Section headings provide structure but are not consistently formatted (e.g., h2 vs h3 vs h4). Apply uniform heading levels to reinforce organization.
Caption for Table 4 refers to Sections A & B but actually shows only Section A & B raw data. Revise caption to reflect content accurately.
Percentage calculation example uses a denominator of 62, but sample size is 61. Reconcile this inconsistency to ensure accurate percentages.
Mean and standard deviation formulas are provided, but no discussion of normality or variance homogeneity assumptions. Add assumption checks before inferential tests.
Frequency table uses overlined notation without legend. Clarify in caption or footnote what overlines denote (e.g., values â¥ average).
Including the breakdown of Section C responses is thorough, enabling detailed comparison between genders. This aids transparency in analytical steps.
Mean and SD table rounds to four decimal places in the body but annotates Â±0.01 and Â±0.001 tolerances. Align rounding conventions throughout for consistency.
Pie Chart 1 and 2 lack a legend explaining slices. Add a legend or labels on chart segments to ensure readers can interpret proportions independently.
Bar Chart 3 shows error bars but the caption doesnât specify whether they represent standard deviation or standard error. Define error bar type for clarity.
The analysis text contains a typo: âmale tend to have a slightly higher mean scores than male.â Correct to âmale tend to have slightly higher mean scores than female.â
Analysis interprets means and SD overlap but doesnât reference t-test results here. Link narrative to inferential outcomes (p-value, t-stat) for stronger interpretation.
Discussion notes low means in C2 and C3 but omits interpretation of environmental significance. Connect awareness levels to actual soil erosion implications.
T-test table uses sample size 9 per group, but earlier analysis included 31 per gender. Clarify why only nine items were analyzed to avoid misinterpretation.
Conclusion correctly accepts Hâ but doesnât discuss the risk of Type II error given the small sample. Suggest adding a power analysis or sample-size justification.
The conclusion links findings to literature but could further evaluate environmental implications beyond gender (e.g., impact on consumption patterns).
Method evaluation notes demographic omission but misses potential Likert-scale biases (central tendency, acquiescence). Discuss these as methodological limitations.
Suggested extension adds colleges from regions but omits discussion of feasibility and sampling logistics. Include a concrete plan for expanding the frame.
The application proposes a government awareness campaign, but lacks analysis of cost, cultural acceptance, or nutritional trade-offs. Strengthen justification with these dimensions.
